ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Singaporean wanton noodles (dry). The closest defensible parent class is 'noodle dish' (FOODON_03311069). A more specific code does not exist in the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_description: Noodles, cooked — used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for this egg-noodle-based composite dish.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for wanton noodles (dry). A0CKP (cooked noodles) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for wanton noodles (dry) as a prepared dish. SNOMED CT coverage of composite Asian dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of dry wanton noodles is approximately 250 g, comprising a portion of egg noodles, two to four wanton dumplings, several slices of char siew, a small portion of chye sim, and a drizzle of sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Wanton noodles (dry) — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for 'Wanton noodles (dry)', CR ID F05060134. All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Noodles, egg, boiled — GI value 58 (analogous estimate for dry-tossed egg noodle dish)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singaporean wanton noodles (dry) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 58 is estimated using boiled egg noodles as the closest analogous food from the University of Sydney GI Database (GI ~58, medium category). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (58) × available carbohydrate per serving (39.15 g from 250 g serving) ÷ 100 = 22.7, rounded to 22.8. The sauce components (soy sauce, oyster sauce) contribute minimal additional carbohydrate. This estimate supersedes the input 'Embedded GI Database' source which is not an approved source under Project Veritas v2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 419 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold; a standard 250 g serving delivers approximately 1,048 mg sodium, representing 44% of the 2,400 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150 g or fewer, request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce, and avoid adding chili sauce or additional condiments to keep sodium intake within safe limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index of 58 and carbohydrate at 15.66 g/100 g; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 39 g available carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~22.8, which is in the high GL range.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit serving size to 150–180 g to keep glycemic load below 15, pair with additional non-starchy vegetables, and request less sauce to reduce hidden sugars from oyster sauce.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 419 mg/100 g and the presence of char siew (BBQ pork) contribute saturated fat and sodium, both of which are risk factors for cardiovascular disease; fat at 6.37 g/100 g is moderate.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request less lard or oil in preparation, substitute char siew with lean chicken or tofu to reduce saturated fat, and limit portion size to control total sodium and fat intake per meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Char siew (BBQ pork, red meat) contains arachidonic acid that amplifies inflammatory pathways; sodium at 419 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g RA threshold, worsening tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request substitution of char siew with chicken or tofu, reduce soy sauce and oyster sauce to lower sodium, and avoid optional chili sauce if nightshade-sensitive.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wanton Noodles (Dry) is a popular Singaporean Chinese hawker dish consisting of springy egg noodles tossed in a savoury sauce made from soy sauce, oyster sauce, sesame oil, and lard, served with boiled wanton dumplings filled with minced pork, sliced char siew (Cantonese-style BBQ pork), and blanched chye sim (Chinese flowering cabbage). Unlike the soup version, the dry variant features noodles coated in a glossy, umami-rich sauce rather than submerged in broth, giving a more concentrated flavour and slightly chewy texture. The dish is a staple of Singapore's hawker culture and is commonly consumed as a breakfast, lunch, or dinner meal. Per 100 g, it provides 149 kcal, 7.15 g protein, 6.37 g fat, and 15.66 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 419 mg — notably high due to the soy and oyster sauce base. The glycemic index is estimated at 58 (medium), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is not suitable for individuals with hypertension without modification due to its high sodium content, and those with cardiovascular disease or rheumatoid arthritis should consider requesting substitutions for char siew. It is not halal-certified due to pork content.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (CR ID F05060134) at confidence 0.95, which is an approved primary source for Singaporean dishes. The GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using boiled egg noodles as the closest defensible proxy, which reduces overall confidence slightly but remains within the 'high' band given the strong primary nutrient source.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB source): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These micronutrient and fatty acid fractions are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for this composite dish as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat of 2.1 g/100 g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for comparable egg noodle and pork composite dishes (SR Legacy), as HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry; this is an approximation and should be verified if precise saturated fat data is required. GI source: the input record cited 'Embedded GI Database' which is not an approved source under Project Veritas v2; this has been replaced with an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using boiled egg noodles (GI ~58). Glycemic load per serving recalculated at 250 g serving size: (58 × 39.15) ÷ 100 = 22.7 (reported as 22.8). The input GI glycemic_load of 9.1 appears to have been calculated on a 100 g basis rather than a realistic serving size and has been corrected. Dietary flag is_halal set to false as the dish contains pork (char siew and wanton filling). Cultural ethnic_tags 'western' from input has been removed as this is a Singaporean Chinese dish with no Western classification basis.
